coredata.dc:title: Bonding Ru(II)-based probe with blue-emitting carbon dots for the optical sensing of aqueous dissolved oxygen: Synthesis and performance

coredata.dc:description: The concentration of dissolved oxygen is an important analyte in the fields of biological research and medical analysis. In this work, the blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a hydrothermal method and used as a supporting host and reference. Three Ru(II) compounds with terpyridine-derived ligands were covalently bonded with the CDs and served as the sensing probes. The resulting composite samples (Run@CDs, n = 1, 2, 3) were analyzed and characterized by morphological analysis, XRD, IR, and elemental analysis. The optimal sample (Ru3@CDs) showed an oxygen sensing feature, with the emission band from the Ru(II) probe efficiently quenched by dissolved oxygen and the one from the CDs insensitive to dissolved oxygen. A linear calibration curve was fitted as I0/I = 0.839 + 0.443*[O2], with an R2 of 0.998, response time of 24 s, and LOD (limit of detection) of 0.34 mg/L. Ru3@CDs showed selectivity toward common interferents, except for H2O2. Its singlet oxygen generation ability was confirmed by the EPR (electron paramagnetic resonance) method. The novelty of this work was the combination of the red emission from the Ru(II) probe and the blue emission from the CDs, which resulted in the emission color change from red ([O2] = 0.52 mg/L) to blue ([O2] = 40.01 mg/L).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Geometric structure of Ru1 (a), Ru2 (b), Ru3 (c), and their stacking mode (d, Ru1; e, Ru2; f, Ru3). See Supporting Information for their detailed geometric parameters.
The simulated absorption spectra (a) and frontier molecular orbitals of Ru1 (b: HOMO, c: LUMO), Ru2 (d: HOMO, e: LUMO), and Ru3 (f: HOMO, g: LUMO) calculated at RB3LYP/LANL2DZ level.
Absorption spectra (a), excitation spectra (b), emission spectra (c), and excited state lifetimes (d) of Ru1, Ru2, Ru3, and CDs dispersed in degassed double-distilled water (10 mg/mL).

XRD patterns (a) of CDs, Ru1@CDs, Ru2@CDs, and Ru3@CDs. SEM images of CDs (b), Ru1@CDs (c), Ru2@CDs (d), and Ru3@CDs (e). A TEM view of CDs (b inset, and more TEM images in the Supporting Information).
IR spectra (a) of CDs, Ru1, Ru2, Ru3, Run@CDs (n = 1, 2, 3). Emission (b), excitation (c, Ru(II) emission was monitored), and excited state lifetimes (d) of Run@CDs (n = 1, 2, 3).

Emission spectra (a) of Ru3@CDs upon various [O2] levels, inset: CIE coordinates of Ru3@CDs with [O2] = 0.52 mg/L (1) and 40.01 mg/L (2), respectively. I0/I vs [O2] and I452/I598 vs [O2] plots and their fitting lines (b), inset: emission intensity values at 452 nm and 598 nm upon various [O2] levels. Emission spectra (c) of Ru3@CDs upon common competing species and interferents (50 μM), including blank (1, [O2] = 0.52 mg/mL]), Ca2+ (2), Mg2+ (3), Fe2+ (4), Fe3+ (5), Co2+ (6), Ni2+ (7), Cu2+ (8), Zn2+ (9), Hg2+ (10), H+ (11), H2O2 (12), GSH (13), and O2 (14, [O2] = 40.01 mg/mL]). Emission monitoring (d) at 598 nm with [O2] increased.

EPR analyses of blank and Ru3@CDs (λex = 344 nm) in O2-saturated aqueous solutions with TEMP (100 mM) as radical scavenger. Insets: the time-dependent absorption spectra of DPBF in MeOH (50 μM), with (up chart) or without (down chart) Ru3@CDs (10 mg/mL, λex = 344 nm).

b, biexponential decay, the weighted mean average value was calculated by (A1τ1 2 + A2τ2 2)/(A1τ1 + A2τ2).

a, the concentrations of dissolved oxygen were determined by the fitted equations and the standard method (Winkler). The reference concentrations (by Winkler method) were determined as 1, 3.53 mg/L; 2, 8.92 mg/L; 3, 35.49 mg/L. Tap water was firstly boiled and then bubbled with N2 stream for 3 min to eliminate the dissolved O2, and then bubbled with O2 stream. The values in “()” were recovery values.

head.ce:title: Bonding Ru(II)-based probe with blue-emitting carbon dots for the optical sensing of aqueous dissolved oxygen: Synthesis and performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Fuyang Normal University, Fuyang, Anhui 236037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Fuyang Normal University, Fuyang, Anhui, 236037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The concentration of dissolved oxygen is an important analyte in the fields of biological research and medical analysis. In this work, the blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a hydrothermal method and used as a supporting host and reference. Three Ru(II) compounds with terpyridine-derived ligands were covalently bonded with the CDs and served as the sensing probes. The resulting composite samples (Run@CDs, n = 1, 2, 3) were analyzed and characterized by morphological analysis, XRD, IR, and elemental analysis. The optimal sample (Ru3@CDs) showed an oxygen sensing feature, with the emission band from the Ru(II) probe efficiently quenched by dissolved oxygen and the one from the CDs insensitive to dissolved oxygen. A linear calibration curve was fitted as I0/I = 0.839 + 0.443*[O2], with an R2 of 0.998, response time of 24 s, and LOD (limit of detection) of 0.34 mg/L. Ru3@CDs showed selectivity toward common interferents, except for H2O2. Its singlet oxygen generation ability was confirmed by the EPR (electron paramagnetic resonance) method. The novelty of this work was the combination of the red emission from the Ru(II) probe and the blue emission from the CDs, which resulted in the emission color change from red ([O2] = 0.52 mg/L) to blue ([O2] = 40.01 mg/L).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, intense research efforts have been devoted to a class of carbon-based materials, carbon dots (CDs), which have shown promising features such as versatile sources, ease of synthesis, low cost, and controllable parameters [1,2]. These CDs have been tried for various applications such as luminescence-based sensing, imaging, and even light-triggered delivery/release [1,2]. There are two types of synthetic methods for the CDs, which are “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods [3–5]. The hydrothermal method is a commonly used one, and the resulting CDs are hydrophilic, which endow these CDs with applications in aqueous media. For example, Zhu and coworkers synthesized hydrophilic CDs using starting materials of citric acid and ethylenediamine for the optical sensing and imaging of aqueous Fe3+ [6].

Dissolved oxygen has been recognized as an important analyte in the fields of biological research and medical analysis. Thus, research efforts have been devoted to the development of optical sensing platforms using the CDs for aqueous dissolved oxygen. Some prior research has been reported. For example, Sun and coworkers developed an optical sensing platform based on the CDs showing an emission turn-on response to dissolved oxygen [7]. Wang and coworkers developed an optical sensing platform for dissolved oxygen using a composite structure of Ru(II)-based emitter and CDs host, where a ratiometric signal was modulated using the emission intensity values of the two structural components [8]. In their work, the emission of the CDs was oxygen-insensitive and thus served as a reference, while that of the Ru(II) probe was oxygen-sensitive and thus used as the probe. The ratiometric Ru(II) emission intensity relative to CDs emission intensity improved sensing selectivity. On the other hand, the examples of ratiometric sensing platforms for dissolved oxygen detection are still limited due to the narrow excitation/emission window of the CDs, which makes the modulation between the CDs and the emitter hard to accomplish [9].

There have been many examples using Ru(II) complexes as sensing probes [8–10]. The major reason is the long-lived excited state derived from the MLCT (metal-to-ligand charge transfer) state, which facilitates sufficient collisions with O2 molecules, enabling energy transfer from the Ru(II) probe to O2, acknowledged as a dynamic quenching mechanism [8–10]. Generally, a longer lifetime of the excited state is desired since it improves the collision probability with O2. In addition, the excitation window of the Ru(II) probe should overlap well with the emission band of the CDs to ensure an efficient energy transfer from the CDs to the Ru(II) probe. Enlightened by the above consideration, in this work, the CDs were synthesized by a classic hydrothermal method using the fibers of Euonymus bungeanus as the starting material owing to their low cost and their long, regular fibers (see Supporting Information for further explanation) [11]. Then, three Ru(II) complexes with terpyridine-derived ligands were prepared. The blue-emitting CDs were covalently bonded with the Ru(II) complexes to form a potential composite nanosensing platform for dissolved oxygen. Here the CDs served as a reference and the Ru(II) complexes served as a probe. The dissolved oxygen was expected to quench the probe emission and thus lead to emission intensity variations, namely an oxygen-sensing signal. The detailed characterization of the Ru(II) complexes (Ru1-Ru3) and the composite structure (Run@CDs, n = 1, 2, 3) offered a clue for the correlation between improved oxygen-sensing performance and probe molecular structure.

Scheme 1 shows the synthetic strategy of the composite structure (Run@CDs, n = 1, 2, 3), along with the molecular structures of the three Ru(II) complexes (Ru1, Ru2, and Ru3). All synthesis-related reagents and materials were analytical grade, commercially obtained, and were used directly without further purification unless otherwise noted, including N-ethylmorpholine, RuCl3·nH2O (CAS 10049–08-8), [2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine]-4′-carboxylic acid (CAS 148332–36-9), 2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine (CAS 1148–79-4), 4′-phenyl-2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine (CAS 58345–97-4), 2,3,5,6-tetra(pyridin-2-yl)pyrazine (CAS 25005–97-4), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (mentioned as EDC, CAS 25952–53-8), N-hydroxysuccinimide (mentioned as NHS, CAS 6066–82-6), 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (mentioned as DPBF, CAS 5471–63-6), and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (mentioned as TEMP, CAS 768–66-1). The fibers of Euonymus bungeanus were dried prior to use. NMR spectra were recorded using a Varian INOVA 300 spectrometer. Single-crystal XRD data were recorded using a Bruker D4 X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα1 radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å, voltage = 40 kV, current = 30 mA). Elemental analysis results were obtained using a Carlo Erba 1106 elemental analyzer. The time-dependent density functional theory calculations were performed on the Ru(II) complexes at the RB3LYP/LANL2DZ level in vacuum using Orca (version 5.04) and Firefly (version 8.2). The initial geometry for the theoretical calculation was first optimized using MOPAC (v22.0.6) using the PM7 Hamilton. Selected frontier molecular orbitals were plotted using wxMacMolPlt (version 7.7.3, contour value = 0.025). Absorption spectra were simulated using Multiwfn (version 3.8) based on the first 100 singlet electronic transitions calculated using Orca (version 5.04). Absorption and emission spectra were experimentally recorded using a Shimadzu UV-3101 PC spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F-7000 spectrometer (luminescence mode, slits = 2 nm × 2 nm), respectively. The excited-state lifetime was determined using an FL920 spectrometer. Emission quantum yields (QYs) were measured using a literature method with a reference of quinine sulfate (in 1.0 M sulfuric acid, QY = 0.546) [12]. Degassed solvents were used unless otherwise noted. CIE coordinates were determined using a SpectraScan PR650 spectrometer. Micromorphology was measured using a Hitachi S-4800 microscope and a JEOL JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded using a Thermo Fisher EscaLab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analyses were performed using a Bruker A300 spectrometer and a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ru1, Ru2, and Ru3
ce:section.ce:para: These Ru(II) complexes were synthesized following literature procedures with minor modifications [13–15]. First, Ru(2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine)Cl3, Ru([2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine]-4′-carboxylic acid)Cl3, and Ru(4′-phenyl-2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine)Cl3 were synthesized as described below [13]. A mixture of anhydrous ethanol (120 mL), 10 mmol of RuCl3·nH2O, and 10 mmol of 2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine (or [2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine]-4′-carboxylic acid, 4′-phenyl-2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine) was placed in a 250 mL round-bottom flask. Under an Ar atmosphere, this mixture was heated at 95 °C for 4 h. After cooling, the crude product was filtered off from the reddish-yellow solution and washed with ethanol to give a brown powder.

Then, 1 mmol of 2,3,5,6-tetra(pyridin-2-yl)pyrazine was dissolved in 80 mL of anhydrous DMF. The solution of Ru([2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine]-4′-carboxylic acid)Cl3 (0.5 mmol) in anhydrous DMF (10 mL) was added dropwise over 2 h into the 2,3,5,6-tetra(pyridin-2-yl)pyrazine solution. The resulting mixture was heated at 135 °C for 6 h under an Ar atmosphere. Then the solvent was extracted under reduced pressure. The red crude product was purified on an Al2O3 column (300 mesh) using CH3CN as eluent to give ([2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine]-4′-carboxylic acid)Ru(2,3,5,6-tetra(pyridin-2-yl)pyrazine)Cl2.

Finally, the ([2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine]-4′-carboxylic acid)Ru(2,3,5,6-tetra(pyridin-2-yl)pyrazine)Cl2 (0.25 mmol) was mixed with ethylene glycol (80 mL) and heated to 185 °C for 12 h under an argon atmosphere. A solution of Ru(2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine)Cl3 (0.25 mmol) in ethylene glycol (80 mL) was added dropwise over 0.5 h. Then the solvent was extracted under reduced pressure. The red crude product was purified on an Al2O3 column (300 mesh) using CH3CN as eluent to give ([2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine]-4′-carboxylic acid)Ru(2,3,5,6-tetra(pyridin-2-yl)pyrazine)Ru(2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine)Cl4 (mentioned as Ru1). Yield: 35 %. 1H NMR (DMSO‑d 6, 300 MHz) δ: 8.65 – 8.37 (m, 10H), 8.18 – 8.07 (m, 2H), 7.95 – 7.82 (m, 8H), 7.72 – 7.41 (m, 12H), 7.38 – 7.20 (m, 5H). 13C NMR (DMSO‑d 6, 75 MHz) δ: 166.58, 153.16, 153.09, 153.06, 152.91, 152.75, 152.73, 151.41, 151.38, 151.35, 151.32, 150.49, 150.45, 150.41, 150.37, 149.10, 149.08, 149.07, 149.06, 148.37, 148.34, 147.94, 147.91, 136.79, 136.72, 136.48, 136.41, 136.33, 132.88, 132.85, 132.82, 131.31, 127.83, 127.75, 127.67, 127.60, 127.25, 127.21, 127.17, 127.06, 127.02, 125.59, 125.51, 125.30, 125.22, 125.17, 125.09, 124.30, 124.28, 124.26. Calculation for C55H38N12O2Ru2Cl4: C, 53.15; H, 3.08; N, 13.52. Found: C, 53.01; H, 3.11; N, 13.41. See its full geometric parameters from the Supporting Information.

([2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine]-4′-carboxylic acid)Ru(2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine)Cl2 (mentioned as Ru3) was synthesized by the second step of synthesis, except that the 2,3,5,6-tetra(pyridin-2-yl)pyrazine was replaced with 2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine. Yield: 59 %. 1H NMR (DMSO‑d 6, 300 MHz) δ: 8.52 – 8.42 (m, 6H), 8.14 (d, J = 8.3 Hz, 2H), 7.88 (td, J = 8.1, 1.5 Hz, 4H), 7.69 – 7.57 (m, 4H), 7.38 – 7.27 (m, 5H). 13C NMR (DMSO‑d 6, 75 MHz) δ: 166.58, 157.81, 157.72, 156.17, 156.14, 155.33, 155.22, 152.46, 152.38, 151.66, 151.58, 148.18, 136.79, 136.72, 136.48, 136.33, 131.31, 127.83, 127.75, 127.67, 127.60, 127.00, 126.97, 125.59, 125.51, 125.30, 125.22, 125.17, 125.09. Calculation for C31H22N6O2Ru1Cl2: C, 54.55; H, 3.25; N, 12.31. Found: C, 54.39; H, 3.17; N, 12.23. See its full geometric parameters from the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method following a literature procedure, except that the fibers of Euonymus bungeanus were used as the starting material in this run [16]. The fibers of Euonymus bungeanus (1.5 g) were cleaned and dried at 45 °C for 24 h before use and then shredded by a ball grinder (1200 rpm for 10 min). The obtained fine powder (0.7 g) was soaked into a mixture of ethylenediamine (1.02 g) and double-distilled water (20 mL), and then treated with an ultrasonic bath for 20 min. The resulting suspension was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave (capacity = 30 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 11 h. After natural cooling, the dark brown suspension was dialyzed using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off = 1000) against double-distilled water (500 mL) for 24 h, and then repeated the dialysis procedure two more times. The obtained product was collected by freeze-drying (yield: 0.12 g), then dispersed in double-distilled water (10 mg/mL), and eventually stored at 4 °C as a stock suspension.

Ru1 (or Ru2 or Ru3, 0.3 g) was weighed and dissolved in double-distilled water (10 mL). This solution was treated with an ultrasonic bath for 15 min, then several drops of dilute HCl were added until the pH reached ∼ 6. Upon vigorous stirring, EDC (0.75 g) and NHS (0.45 g) were added, and then the resulting mixture was allowed to react at ambient conditions for 3 h. Finally, the CDs suspension (10 mg/mL) was added dropwise over 0.5 h. The mixture was stirred at ambient conditions for 15 h, and then centrifuged for 12 min (× 5500). The upper layer was dialyzed using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off = 1000) against double-distilled water (500 mL) for 24 h, then the dialysis procedure was repeated 4 more times. The obtained product was collected by freeze-drying (yield: 0.09 g), dispersed in degassed double-distilled water (10 mg/mL), and stored at 4 °C as a stock suspension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: How to determine the concentration of dissolved molecular oxygen by Run@CDs (n = 1, 2, 3)
ce:section.ce:para: For the measurement of steady emission spectra (such as the sensing spectra), the oxygen levels were controlled as follows. N2 stream was bubbled into double-distilled water to remove the dissolved oxygen. Then the flows of N2 and O2 streams were controlled by gas meters and mixed together at desired ratios, and finally imported into the test samples to adjust the concentration of dissolved oxygen. Each test sample was equally divided into two parts, and individually sealed into two Winkler bottles. One bottle was determined by a standard procedure (GB7489-87, China), and the other bottle was mixed with Run@CDs so that its concentration of dissolved oxygen could be determined by the optical sensing method. For the measurement of dynamic response (such as the sensing response), the emission intensity of a sample with a low level of dissolved oxygen (<1 mg/L) was continuously monitored when another sample with a high level of dissolved oxygen (∼40 mg/L) was added quickly. The sensing response time was determined using the Hitachi F-7000 spectrometer (with “Time Scan” mode, monitoring wavelength = 598 nm). A preliminary correction method is given in Supporting Information if there are oxidative or reductive interferents in a testing sample.

For the EPR experiment, TEMP (2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine) was added into O2-saturated aqueous solutions. Then Ru3@CDs was added (10 mg/mL). After being irradiated (λex = 344 nm) for 15 min, the sample was sent for EPR analysis. A reference experiment was performed similarly, except that no Ru3@CDs was added. For the DPBF (1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran) measurement, a stock solution of DPBF in methanol (50 μM, 10 mL) was prepared. After being saturated with O2 bubbles for 15 min, Ru3@CDs was added (10 mg/mL). The mixture was continuously irradiated (λex = 344 nm). A controlled volume of solution was extracted every two minutes and centrifuged (5500 rpm) for 5 min. The upper transparent layer was sent for absorption measurement. A reference experiment was performed similarly, except that no Ru3@CDs was added.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization on Ru1, Ru2, Ru3, and CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic route and working strategy of Run@CDs (n = 1, 2, 3) are presented as Scheme 1. A hydrothermal method was applied to synthesize the carbon dots (CDs). During this hydrothermal synthesis procedure, ethylenediamine served as a stabilizer and thus covered the resulting carbon dots. Consequently, the surface of the as-synthesized CDs is loaded with –NH2 groups. These –NH2 groups allowed covalent bonding with the –COOH groups of the Ru(II) compounds, forming a composite nanosensing platform. The emission from the Ru(II) compounds is supposed to be quenched by molecular oxygen (3O2) via dynamic collisions and thus serves as the sensing probe. Upon receiving the energy from the probe, the molecular oxygen is excited, yielding singlet oxygen (1O2), as depicted in Scheme 1. While the emission from the CDs is anticipated to be insensitive to molecular oxygen, and acts as a reference. As shown in Fig. 1 , Ru1 and Ru2 are dual-cored compounds with two Ru(II) ions in each molecule, and each Ru(II) ion is coordinated by two tridentate ligands ([2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine]-4′-carboxylic acid and 2,3,5,6-tetra(pyridin-2-yl)pyrazine/4′-phenyl-2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine), forming a traditional octahedral field [13,14]. Ru3 is a mono-cored compound with a traditional octahedral field formed by two tridentate ligands (2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine and [2,2′:6′,2′'-terpyridine]-4′-carboxylic acid) [13,14]. The Cl anions are localized far away from the coordination field, which makes them only counterions instead of coordinating ligands. Due to the restriction and geometric hindrance of the multiple aromatic rings of Ru1 and Ru2, Ru1 and Ru2 molecules are far away from each other in each unit cell, while Ru3 molecules tend to form a regular stacking, as shown in Fig. 1. The two dual-cored compounds (Ru1 and Ru2) are expected to have improved energy transfer efficiency by harvesting more excitation energy from the CDs compared to the mono-cored Ru3, as will be discussed later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic transitions and simulated absorption spectra of Ru1, Ru2, and Ru3
ce:section.ce:para: According to the literature reports and conclusions, the Ru(II)-based probes for oxygen sensing generally have MLCT (metal-to-ligand-charge-transfer) transitions [10,17,18]. Because of the participation of metal d orbitals in the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) of the Ru(II)-based probes, the radiative transition probability is relatively small, leading to a long-lived excited state (on the scale of several microseconds), which allows enough dynamic collision opportunities with oxygen molecules, thus quenching the excited state of the Ru(II)-based probes and showing quenched emission, which serves as the sensing signal [17,18]. To reveal the electronic transitions in Ru1, Ru2, and Ru3, their selected FMOs are calculated at the RB3LYP/LANL2DZ level and plotted in Fig. 2 . It is observed that their occupied FMOs are generally composed of metal d orbitals, with minor contributions from the terpyridine-derived ligands. Their unoccupied FMOs, however, are mainly composed of the terpyridine-based ligands, admixed with contributions from the metal d orbitals. Thus, the electronic transitions from the occupied FMOs to the unoccupied ones are expected to have a mixed character of MLCT (metal-to-ligand-charge-transfer) and MC (metal-centered) transitions [17,18]. Due to the limitation of the law of spin selection, MC transitions are highly prohibited, which slows down the radiative decay of the excited state of the Ru(II) complexes, showing a lifetime on a scale of several microseconds. On the positive side, the participation of the MC transition makes the excited state of the Ru(II) complexes long-lived, giving enough collision opportunities between the Ru(II) excited state and oxygen molecules. On the negative side, however, the emission quantum yield is decreased due to the participation of the MC transition. It is still observed that, owing to the electron-withdrawing character of 2,3,5,6-tetra(pyridin-2-yl)pyrazine, the LUMOs of Ru1 and Ru2 are mainly composed of the 2,3,5,6-tetra(pyridin-2-yl)pyrazine ligand, with no obvious contribution from terpyridine-derived ligands. In other words, the excited electrons are surrounded and shielded by the outer terpyridine-derived ligands, which makes it hard for O2 molecules to attack/quench the excited electrons. This is a negative factor for oxygen sensing. As for Ru3, the excited electrons are open for O2 molecules, favoring oxygen sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of Ru1, Ru2, Ru3, and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The key photophysical parameters of Ru1, Ru2, Ru3, and CDs are recorded and listed in Table 1 , including absorption peaks (λabs), excitation peaks (λex), emission peaks (λem), FWHM (full-width-at-half-maximum), emission quantum yields (ϕ), lifetimes (τ), radiative (kr) and non-radiative (knr) probabilities. Fig. 3 shows the absorption spectra of the three Ru(II) compounds and the CDs. The absorption spectra of Ru1 and Ru2 are rather similar to each other due to their similar molecular structures, showing MLCT-based transitions at 538 nm and π-π* transitions at approximately 270 nm [17,18]. The extra benzene ring in the terpyridine-derived ligand of Ru2, compared to Ru1, exerts no obvious effect on the MLCT absorption. This is because, as mentioned in Section 3.1.2, the MLCT transition mainly involves metal d orbitals (in HOMO) and 2,3,5,6-tetra(pyridin-2-yl)pyrazine (in LUMO), with no obvious contribution from terpyridine-derived ligands. Thus, the modification of the terpyridine-derived ligand caused no obvious spectral shifts. As for Ru3, its absorption spectrum is generally similar to those of Ru1 and Ru2, but the MLCT absorption band (452 nm) is blue-shifted because the conjugation size of the Ru3 ligand is smaller than those of Ru1 and Ru2. The CDs exhibit a broad absorption spectrum without distinct peaks, indicating an efficient photon-absorbing system.

Ru1, Ru2, and Ru3 exhibit three excitation peaks that generally match their absorption bands, as shown in Fig. 3 and Table 1. It is still observed that the excitation efficiency of the MLCT transitions (523 nm for Ru1, 527 nm for Ru2, and 422 nm for Ru3) is much higher than that of ligand π-π* transitions (∼350 nm and ∼ 280 nm), even though the absorbance of the MLCT transitions is lower than that of ligand π-π* transitions. A possible reason for this result is that the energy transfer pathways of ligand π-π* and MLCT transitions to the emissive center differ. To be specific, the MLCT excited state efficiently transfers its energy to the emissive state since the emissive state is derived from the MLCT excited state, while ligand π-π* excited state must go through a series of energy dissipation processes before transferring its energy to the emissive state, such as internal conversion and intersystem crossing, leading to its low excitation efficiency [19]. As for the CDs, there are two distinct excitation bands, peaking at 344 nm and 249 nm, respectively. This observation indicates an energy gap between the conduction and valence bands in the CDs. In addition, the first excitation band (344 nm) of the CDs matches the secondary excitation band of the three Ru(II) compounds (around 350 nm). Thus, for subsequent measurements, an excitation wavelength of 344 nm was selected.

The emission spectra of Ru1, Ru2, Ru3, and CDs are shown in Fig. 3. The three Ru(II) compounds exhibit single Gaussian-type emission bands with no shoulder bands. Due to the large conjugation plane in Ru1 and Ru2, their emission wavelength falls in the near-infrared region, peaking at 712 nm for Ru1 and 717 nm for Ru2, respectively. As for Ru3, its emission localizes at a shorter wavelength of 592 nm, owing to its smaller conjugation plane compared to Ru1 and Ru2. Due to their MLCT-based emissive state, the emission bands of these Ru(II) compounds are broad, with FWHM (full-width-at-half-maximum) values of 76–81 nm [17–19]. As for the CDs, their emission band, peaking at 447 nm, is even wider than that of the three Ru(II) compounds, with an FWHM value of 91 nm. This broad emission band partially overlaps with the excitation bands of the three Ru(II) compounds, suggesting a possible energy transfer from the CDs to the Ru(II) compounds. This topic will be discussed and confirmed below in Section 3.2.3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission quantum yields and lifetimes of Ru1, Ru2, Ru3, and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The emission quantum yields (ϕ) of Ru1, Ru2, Ru3, and CDs are listed in Table 1 for convenience of comparison. The blue emission of the CDs has a high ϕ of 21.1 %, making the CDs an efficient energy donor for the excitation of the three Ru(II) compounds. Ru1 and Ru2, however, have low emission quantum yields (5.4 % for Ru1 and 5.2 % for Ru2). As for Ru3, its emission quantum yield is determined to be 9.7 %, slightly higher than those of Ru1 and Ru2. A possible reason is the decreased participation of the MC transition in the FMOs of Ru3, compared to those of Ru1 and Ru2 [17–19]. As mentioned in Section 3.1.2, the emission quantum yield is decreased by the participation of the MC transition. Another possible reason is that the dual-cored structure of Ru1 and Ru2 has a negative effect on the emissive center. When one of the two Ru(II) centers is excited, its energy may be transferred and thus partially quenched by the other Ru(II) center, leading to a compromised emission quantum yield.

The excited state lifetimes of Ru1, Ru2, Ru3, and CDs are recorded and presented in Fig. 3. The emission of the CDs follows a biexponential decay mode with a lifetime of 28.9 ns, which is comparable to literature values [5]. All three Ru(II) compounds exhibit a biexponential decay mode with lifetimes on the order of hundreds of nanoseconds. Compared to the literature values of MLCT-based emitters such as phosphorescent Cu(I) complexes and Re(I) complexes (on the scale of several microseconds), the lifetimes of these Ru(II) compounds are obviously shorter, which can be attributed to the participation of the MC transition, as mentioned above [20,21]. The lifetime of Ru2 (314.8 ns) is slightly shorter than that of Ru1 (320.5 ns), which should be caused by the vibrations of the extra benzene ring in the terpyridine-derived ligand of Ru2, compared to Ru1. The lifetime of Ru3 (374.5 ns) is even longer than the lifetimes of Ru1 and Ru2. This result is consistent with the aforementioned hypothesis that the dual-cored structure of Ru1 and Ru2 has a negative effect on the emissive center. To gain more insight into the excited state, the corresponding radiative and non-radiative constants (kr and knr) are calculated using Equation (1) and Equation (2).
(1)
τ = 1 / (

As listed in Table 1, the kr values of Ru1 (1.69 × 105 s−1) and Ru2 (1.65 × 105 s−1) are similar, owing to their similar molecular structures. The kr value of Ru3 (2.59 × 105 s−1) is higher than those of Ru1 and Ru2. This result suggests that the electron-withdrawing effect of 2,3,5,6-tetra(pyridin-2-yl)pyrazine reduces the electronic transition probability in Ru1 and Ru2. The knr values of Ru1, Ru2, and Ru3, however, are over tenfold higher than their kr values, owing to the participation of the spin-forbidden MC transition, as aforementioned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD and morphology (SEM, TEM, and DLS)
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD patterns of the three Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples are recorded and compared with that of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , there is a broad peak at ∼ 24° in the CDs sample, and this peak has been assigned to the diffraction of (002) according to a literature conclusion, which means that the carbon atoms form an amorphous phase without long-range and well-resolved lattice features [6]. After loading the Ru(II) compounds, this broad XRD peak is still observed in the three Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples without any obvious spectral shift. No XRD peaks from the Ru(II) compounds are observed. As a result, it is concluded that the CDs and their crystal lattice have been well preserved after loading the Ru(II) probe. The micromorphology of the three Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples, along with that of the as-synthesized CDs, is revealed by their SEM and TEM images. As shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S1 (Supporting Information), spherical carbon dots with a mean diameter of ∼ 1 nm are mono-dispersed on the supporting substrate. No significant aggregation is observed. This is because, as mentioned in Section 3.1.1, the surface of the as-synthesized CDs is loaded with –NH2 groups (from the stabilizer, ethylenediamine, used during synthesis), which prevents the carbon dots from aggregating. After loading the Ru(II) probe (Ru1, Ru2, or Ru3), the resulting particles (Run@CD, n = 1–3) grow slightly but remain homogeneously distributed without further aggregation. This monodispersity favors the oxygen-sensing performance by allowing smooth and efficient oxygen diffusion. The DLS analysis of the CDs and three Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples is shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information). The hydration radii are determined as 14.2 nm for the CDs, 15.4 nm for Ru1@CDs, 15.2 nm for Ru2@CDs, and 14.8 nm for Ru3@CDs, respectively. The hydration effect makes these particle radii larger than those obtained from the above morphology analysis. The introduction of ionized Ru(II) probes increased the hydration radii.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probe loading level, XPS, and status via IR spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The Ru(II) probe loading levels in the three Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples are tentatively determined from their elemental analysis results, using that of the as-synthesized CDs as a reference. The C, H, N levels are recorded as 81.36 %, 11.22 %, and 1.50 %, respectively, for the CDs, while the corresponding values are determined to be 78.12 %, 10.24 %, and 2.91 % for Ru1@CDs; 78.54 %, 10.31 %, and 2.81 % for Ru2@CDs; and 77.45 %, 10.01 %, and 3.11 % for Ru3@CDs, respectively. With the molecular formulas of the three Ru(II) probes on hand, their doping levels can be mathematically calculated as 11.5 % for Ru1@CDs, 10.9 % for Ru2@CDs, and 14.6 % for Ru3@CDs. All these doping levels, especially the first two values, are lower than the literature values of Ru(II)-doped porous structures (>20 %) [10,17,18]. It is thus assumed that the Ru(II) probe molecules are attached to the surface of the CDs. The Ru(II) doping level of Ru3@CDs (14.6 %) is higher than those of Ru1@CDs (11.5 %) and Ru2@CDs (10.9 %), which means that the steric hindrance of the dual-cored Ru1 and Ru2 decreases their covalent grafting to the surface of the CDs.

The XPS spectra of the CDs and three Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples are shown in Fig. S3 (Supporting Information). For the CDs, a major peak of 284.6 eV is observed for C 1 s, ending at 288.9 eV, which corresponds to C-C/C=C and C-O/C-N groups. The peaks of N 1 s and O 1 s are 399.6 eV and 532.9 eV, respectively, which belong to –NH2 and C-O groups. After loading the Ru(II) probes, the major peak of 284.6 eV is preserved for Run@CDs (n = 1–3) C 1 s, and a shoulder peak of 287.6 eV is observed, which belongs to the introduced C=O and C=N groups from the Ru(II) probes. The N 1 s peaks of Run@CDs (n = 1–3) range from 398.6 eV to 399.9 eV, which correspond to the azyl and C=N groups. The O 1 s peaks of Run@CDs (n = 1–3) slightly shift to 532.4 eV due to the contribution from C=O (530.7 eV). These assignments match the nature of Ru-modified N-doped carbon dots.

The covalent bonding between the Ru(II) probe and the CDs can be discussed by comparing their IR spectra to those of the three Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples. As shown in Fig. 5 , the IR spectrum of the CDs is a simple one with several characteristic IR peaks. The broad IR band at 3392 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of the –OH from adsorbed water. A group of IR peaks around 2974 cm−1 is observed and assigned to the stretching vibration of –CH groups (from the stabilizer of ethylenediamine) [6,10,17]. The sharp IR peak at 1703 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of the –NH2 group. The three Ru(II) probes exhibit similar IR spectra owing to their similar molecular structures. Their IR peaks at 3435 cm−1 and 1732 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of their –COOH groups. For the three Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples, the IR peaks from the –CH groups (∼2974 cm−1) are observed, but those of the –COOH groups (3435 cm−1 and 1732 cm−1) greatly decrease (in Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs) or even disappear (in Ru3@CDs). While the IR peaks from the –CO-NH group at 1609 cm−1 are clearly observed. This result indicates that the Ru(II) probe has been covalently bonded with the CDs via a chemical bond between the –COOH group (from the Ru(II) probe) and the –NH2 group (from the CDs). There is still a trace signal for the IR peak of the –NH2 group at 1703 cm−1 for Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs, but not for Ru3@CDs. This is because, as aforementioned, the probe doping level of Ru3@CDs is higher than those of Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs, due to the small steric hindrance of probe Ru3, compared to Ru1 and Ru2. As a consequence, there may be residual –NH2 groups in Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission, excitation, excited state lifetime, and FRET
ce:section.ce:para:
As aforementioned in Section 3.1.3, the broad emission spectrum of the CDs (FWHM = 91 nm) partially overlaps with the excitation bands of the three Ru(II) compounds and thus may transfer energy to them via a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. According to a literature conclusion, the FRET efficiency is controlled and affected by multiple factors, such as Q0 (donor intrinsic ϕ), J (spectral overlap integral between donor emission and acceptor absorption/excitation), κ2 (mutual molecular orientation), nd (solvent refractive index), NA (Avogadro’s number), λ (wavelength in nm), fd(λ) (donor emission intensity), εA(λ) (acceptor molar extinction coefficient), as described by Equation (3) and Equation (4) [22].
(3)
R 0 6
=
9
Q 0
k 2
J
ln 10
128
π 5
n d 4
N A
(4)
J = ∫
f D
λ
ε A
λ
λ 4
d λ
With these two equations, it is assumed that the spectral overlap integral J exerts a major effect on the FRET efficiency. Given the obvious spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the CDs and the excitation spectra of the three Ru(II) compounds, it is possible that there is efficient energy transfer from the CDs to the Ru(II) probe.

To confirm the above assumption, the excitation and emission spectra of the three Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples are recorded and shown in Fig. 5. All three Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples exhibit typical emission bands from the two structural components, peaking at 452 nm from the CDs, 716 nm from Ru1/Ru2, and 598 nm from Ru3. Compared to the emission bands of the CDs (447 nm) and the Ru(II) compounds (Ru1, 712 nm; Ru2, 717 nm; Ru3, 592 nm), the emission bands of the Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples are red-shifted. The covalent bonding between the CDs and the Ru(II) compounds spreads the electron cloud and thus decreases the excited-state energy, leading to emission redshift. For Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs, the relative emission intensity of the CDs is much stronger than that of Ru1/Ru2. In contrast, for Ru3@CDs, the relative emission intensity of the CDs is much lower than that of Ru3. There are two possible reasons for this observation. First, the intrinsic emission quantum yields of Ru1 (5.4 %) and Ru2 (5.2 %) are lower than that of Ru3 (9.7 %). Thus, given the same FRET efficiency, the low relative emission intensity of the CDs in Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs is reasonable compared to Ru3@CDs. Second, the FRET process may be compromised by the dual-cored structure of the probes in Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs, leading to low excitation efficiency of the Ru(II) emissive center.

To find out the major reason for the low relative emission intensity of the CDs in Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs, the excitation spectra of the three Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples are recorded and compared to those of the CDs, Ru1, Ru2, and Ru3. As shown in Fig. 5, each excitation spectrum of the Run@CDs is composed of three excitation bands peaking at ∼ 250 nm, ∼347 nm, and 525 nm (for Ru1 and Ru2) or 432 nm (for Ru3). The former two bands match the excitation spectrum of the CDs well, while the latter band matches the excitation spectra of the Ru(II) compounds. A slight red shift (< 10 nm) is observed compared to those of the CDs and the three Ru(II) compounds due to the covalent bonding between the CDs and the Ru(II) probe, which spreads the electron cloud and thus decreases the excited-state energy, as mentioned above. The observation of effective excitation for the Ru(II) emissive center at ∼ 250 nm and ∼ 347 nm, corresponding to the excitation of the CDs, indicates efficient energy transfer from the CDs to the Ru(II) emissive center.

To strengthen this statement, the excited state lifetimes (τ’) of the three Run@CDs (n = 1–3) samples are recorded and compared to those of Ru1, Ru2, and Ru3. The emission of these three samples still follows a biexponential decay mode, with lifetimes of 462.7 ns for Ru1@CDs, 358.0 ns for Ru2@CDs, and 510.6 ns for Ru3@CDs, respectively. These values are obviously longer than those of Ru1 (320.5 ns), Ru2 (314.8 ns), and Ru3 (374.5 ns). On the other hand, the lifetime of the CDs decreases to 25.0 ns for Ru1@CDs, 22.7 ns for Ru2@CDs, and 15.7 ns for Ru3@CDs, respectively, compared to the intrinsic lifetime of CDs (28.9 ns). This confirms that energy is transferred from the CDs to the Ru(II) probe. Corresponding energy transfer efficiency (η) can be calculated by Equation (5). Here, τ’ means the lifetime of donor (the CDs) in the presence of acceptor (the Ru(II) probe), and τ denotes the intrinsic donor (the CDs) lifetime without acceptor.
(5)
η = 1 -
τ ′
/ τ

The η values are calculated to be 13.5 % for Ru1@CDs, 21.5 % for Ru2@CDs, and 45.7 % for Ru3@CDs, respectively. It is concluded that the energy transfer efficiency from the CDs to the Ru(II) probe in Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs is lower than that in Ru3@CDs, which might be caused by the different spectral overlaps between the CDs’ emission and the Ru(II) compounds’ excitation. Combined with the lower emission quantum yields of Ru1 and Ru2, compared to Ru3, it is reasonable to observe the low probe emission in Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs.

As mentioned above, the relative emission intensity of the Ru(II) probe is weak in Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs due to the low emission quantum yields of their probes and the low energy transfer efficiency to their probes. This weak emission intensity makes Ru1@CDs and Ru2@CDs unsuitable for further oxygen sensing applications. Consequently, only Ru3@CDs was tested for oxygen sensing applications.

The emission spectra of Ru3@CDs soaked in solutions with different levels of dissolved O2 (denoted as [O2]) were recorded and analyzed to evaluate its oxygen sensing performance. As shown in Fig. 6 , at a low level of dissolved oxygen ([O2] = 0.25 mg/L), two emission bands are clearly observed: a strong one from the Ru3 probe peaking at 598 nm and a relatively weak one from the CDs peaking at 452 nm. As [O2] increases, the probe emission intensity (at 598 nm) decreases significantly. At a high [O2] level of 40.01 mg/L, the probe emission is almost completely quenched. This result confirms that the probe emission is highly sensitive to O2. Meanwhile, the CDs emission intensity (at 452 nm) slightly increases. This is because the lifetime of the CDs emission is too short (28.9 ns, Table 1) for oxygen to effectively quench it [10,17,18]. Additionally, the quenching effect on the Ru(II) emissive center suppresses energy transfer from the CDs to the Ru(II) probe, leading to an increase in the emission intensity of the CDs at 452 nm. It is noteworthy that, despite the clearly quenched emission intensity of the Ru(II) probe, no new emission bands or spectral shifts occur, indicating a dynamic sensing mechanism based on a collision between the Ru(II) probe and the oxygen molecules, as presented in Equation (6).
(6)
3O2 + Ru3*→1O2 + Ru3
“*” indicates an excited-state.

At various [O2] levels, the relative intensity variation of the red emission from the Ru(II) probe compared to the well-preserved blue emission from the CDs causes a change in emission color from red ([O2] = 0.52 mg/L) to blue ([O2] = 40.01 mg/L), as depicted in Fig. 6.

Then the sensing response of Ru3@CDs towards various levels of dissolved oxygen is then analyzed with the Stern-Volmer equation [10,17,18]. When a probe is uniformly distributed in a homogeneous host and follows a dynamic quenching mechanism, the intensity form of the Stern-Volmer equation is given by Equation (7). In this equation, I represents the emission intensity, I0 is the initial intensity without any quencher, KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [O2] is the concentration of dissolved oxygen, respectively.
(7)
I 0
/ I = 1 +
K
SV
∙
O 2
An ideal working calibration of I0/I versus [O2] should be a straight line with a slope of KSV . As shown in Fig. 6, the I0/I plots follow a linear correlation with [O2], with a fitting equation of I0/I = 0.839 + 0.443*[O2], R2 = 0.998. The LOD (limit of detection, 3σ/N) value is determined to be 0.34 mg/L. Using the emission intensity of the CDs at 452 nm as a reference (I452), the Stern-Volmer equation is modified to Equation 8, with a fitting equation of I452/I598 = -0.152 + 0.08*[O2], R2 = 0.996. The corresponding LOD value (3σ/N) is determined to be 3.15 mg/L. This working calibration (I452/I598 versus [O2]) is clearly less effective than the standard one (I0/I versus [O2]).

The accuracy of an optical sensing platform depends on the unique sensing response towards the analyte, which is known as selectivity. In practical cases, the analyte is usually dispersed in a complex environment containing competing species and interferants, leading to false responses and reduced accuracy. To evaluate the selectivity of Ru3@CDs, its emission spectra in the presence of several representative competing species and interferents are recorded. These include Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, H+, H2O2, and glutathione (GSH). As observed in Fig. 6, the emission bands of Ru3@CDs are not affected by most interferents with a closed-shell structure, such as Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, and GSH. The corresponding I0/I values (0.98–1.04) are much lower than that in the presence of O2 (I0/I = 16.5). In the presence of metal cations with an open-shell structure, such as Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, and Hg2+, the emission bands of Ru3@CDs are significantly quenched, with I0/I values ranging from 1.11 to 1.34. This is because the open-shell structure serves as an efficient energy-harvesting system, thereby quenching the emission bands of Ru3@CDs. Nevertheless, these I0/I values are still much lower than that in the presence of O2 (I0/I = 16.5). H2O2 is found to be the strongest interferent among those mentioned, showing an I0/I value of 9.40. This is because H2O2 releases molecular O2, which quenches the emission of Ru3@CDs in the same way as dissolved O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Signal stability, response time and practical performance
ce:section.ce:para: Given that the sensing performance of Ru3@CDs is evaluated through its emission intensity variation in the presence of an analyte, the emission stability of Ru3@CDs thus should be assessed. Fig. 6 shows the intensity monitoring at a wavelength of 598 nm as [O2] increases. It is observed that the red emission intensity remains steady (within 0–45 s) when [O2] is low, indicating good signal stability. The robust nature of the CDs and Ru3 likely contributes to this excellent photostability. Furthermore, the indirect excitation mechanism in Ru3@CDs (where the CDs harvest photons and transfer energy to the Ru(II) probe, instead of exciting the Ru(II) probe directly) may also enhance the signal stability. As [O2] increases, the red emission intensity decreases rapidly (within 45 s-69 s) and is then well preserved (after 70 s). The response time is as short as 24 s, which compares favorably with the literature values for optical sensing platforms detecting gaseous O2 [10,17,18]. This is because there is a strong interaction between the probe and the dissolved oxygen molecules, similar to the case in gaseous sensing.

After discussing the sensing performance of Ru3@CDs, including working calibrations, sensitivity, LOD, selectivity, and response time, its practical sensing performance is tentatively evaluated, with the results listed in Table 2 . With the working calibration of I0/I vs [O2], positive errors are observed, with relative errors of + 10 % or less. Similar positive errors are observed for the calibration of I452/I598 vs [O2]. A possible explanation is the presence of competing species in the practical samples, such as metal cations, which quench the probe emission, leading to false signals and resulting in positive errors. A sensing performance comparison between this work and literature reports is shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information). Ru3@CDs exhibits acceptable sensing performance, and its ratiometric sensing caused an emission color change from red ([O2] = 0.52 mg/L) to blue ([O2] = 40.01 mg/L), which is the attractive feature of this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ru3@CDs as a photosensitizer to yield singlet oxygen (1O2)
ce:section.ce:para: It has been mentioned in Section 3.3.1 that the oxygen sensing process of Ru3@CDs is accompanied by the release of singlet oxygen (1O2, excited state), which has been reported as a ROS (reactive oxygen species) in the applications of wastewater treatment, photochemical synthesis, and photodynamic therapy [23,24]. After above confirming the promising oxygen sensing performance of Ru3@CDs, it is rational to assume that it can produce 1O2 efficiently. First, the released 1O2 is identified by the EPR (electron paramagnetic resonance) analysis shown in Fig. 7 . Using TEMP as a radical scavenger, a representative EPR signal of 1O2 is observed in the presence of Ru3@CDs, confirming the release of 1O2 during the oxygen sensing activity [25]. Then the 1O2-releasing performance of Ru3@CDs is evaluated using DPBF (1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran) as an indicator. Here DPBF serves as a reductive reagent to react with the released 1O2, and the absorbance of DPBF at 415 nm disappears after it is oxidized by 1O2, which is consequently a signal of 1O2 [26]. As shown in Fig. 7, the DPBF absorbance at 415 nm completely disappears after ten minutes of radiation, with Ru3@CDs as the photosensitizer. As a contrast, 94.0 % of the initial absorbance is preserved after being radiated for 24 min in the absence of photosensitizer. This result suggests that Ru3@CDs is a promising photosensitizer for further applications.

In conclusion, three composite samples were synthesized by combining three Ru(II) compounds with terpyridine-derived ligands as probes and blue-emitting CDs as the supporting host and reference. The Ru(II) compounds were discussed in detail, covering their molecular structures, absorption, emission, excitation properties, quantum yields, and lifetimes. The composite samples were then analyzed and characterized using micromorphology analysis, XRD, IR, and elemental analysis. The optimal sample, Ru3@CDs, demonstrated effective oxygen sensing, with the emission from the Ru(II) probe efficiently quenched by dissolved oxygen, while the emission from the CDs remained unaffected by dissolved oxygen. A linear working calibration was obtained as I0/I = 0.839 + 0.443*[O2], with an R2 of 0.998, a response time of 24 s, and an LOD (limit of detection) of 0.34 mg/L. Ru3@CDs exhibited good selectivity towards common interferents, except for H2O2. Its singlet oxygen generation ability was confirmed by EPR method. The novelty of this work was the combination of the red emission from the Ru(II) probe and the blue emission from the CDs, which resulted in the emission color change from red ([O2] = 0.52 mg/L) to blue ([O2] = 40.01 mg/L). However, some challenges remain to be addressed in future work. First, the emission quantum yield of the Ru(II) probe and energy transfer efficiency between the CDs and the Ru(II) probe should be improved to enhance the probe’s emission intensity. Second, further improvements are needed in sensing selectivity and sensitivity.
Shigang Li: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Xiaoyun Zhao: Investigation.
